docs.title: Software Engineer Roadmaps
docs.text: A complete, opinionated guide to software engineering career paths — from first job to staff level, updated for the AI era.

docs.title: What's inside
docs.text:
RoadmapsAI Era SkillsDeep Dive

The foundational skill stages for each engineering path. Organized into four levels:

  • Foundation — baseline skills every engineer on that track needs
  • Core skills — the day-to-day tools you'll use on the job
  • Advanced — what separates mid-level from senior engineers
  • Specialization — deep expertise that defines staff/principal-level work

How each path changes in the age of AI. For every track you'll find:

  • New AI-era skills — things you need that didn't exist 3 years ago
  • Amplified importance — skills that matter more because of AI
  • Still essential — the fundamentals that aren't going anywhere
  • Increasingly automated — where AI handles the boilerplate

The full picture for each path:

  • Career levels (Junior → Mid → Senior → Staff/Principal)
  • Tools ecosystem (core vs. useful-to-know)
  • Sequenced learning path with resource recommendations
  • Interview topic breakdown
  • Salary ranges by level (US market, 2024–2025)

docs.title: The 8 paths
docs.text:
Path Focus Best for Frontend UI, browsers, user experience Visual thinkers who love fast feedback loops Backend APIs, databases, distributed systems Systems thinkers who like working under the hood Full Stack End-to-end product development Generalists who want to ship complete features DevOps / Cloud Infrastructure, reliability, CI/CD Engineers who want to make systems run smoothly Data / ML AI, analytics, machine learning Math-oriented engineers excited about AI Mobile iOS, Android, cross-platform Engineers who want to ship apps people carry everywhere Security AppSec, pentesting, AI security Engineers drawn to adversarial thinking Eng Manager People, strategy, org design Senior engineers ready to lead teams

Cross-cutting advice

Git, a scripting language, and fundamentals (networking, data structures, HTTP) appear in almost every path. They're the connective tissue regardless of direction. Master these first.

The jump from Junior → Mid is about owning full features. From Mid → Senior is about owning systems. From Senior → Staff is about owning people and direction.

docs.title: AI Era Skills — Overview
docs.text: How AI is reshaping every engineering path. For each track, skills are classified into four categories.

docs.title: How to read these pages
docs.text:
New AI-era skills

Things you need that didn't exist or weren't mainstream 3 years ago. These are additive — new skills on top of existing ones.

Amplified importance

Skills that matter more because of AI. When AI handles boilerplate, judgment and architecture become more valuable, not less.

Still essential

The fundamentals that aren't going anywhere. These remain the foundation regardless of AI's progress.

Increasingly automated

Where AI handles the routine work. These skills don't disappear, but you stop getting much credit for them — they become table stakes.

docs.title: Big-picture themes
docs.text:
The skills being automated are rarely disappearing entirely — they're becoming things AI handles in seconds, so you stop getting credit for them. Value moves up the stack to judgment, architecture, and the ability to evaluate AI output critically.

The skills marked amplified are getting harder to fake. When AI writes the boilerplate, the things that separate engineers are taste, system-level thinking, security awareness, and the ability to spot when generated code is subtly wrong.

The cross-cutting skill that appears in every path is the ability to design and evaluate systems that include AI as a component — not just call an API and hope for the best.

docs.title: Path summaries
docs.text: Path Biggest new skill Biggest risk of automation Frontend AI UI tools, streaming patterns Boilerplate component code Backend RAG architecture, LLM APIs Boilerplate CRUD endpoints Full Stack AI feature integration end-to-end Standard UI scaffolding DevOps GPU infra, model serving, LLMOps Boilerplate IaC templates Data / ML Fine-tuning, RAG, evals, agents Standard EDA templates Mobile On-device ML, voice AI Boilerplate list/detail screens Security LLM security, AI red teaming Routine vulnerability scans Eng Manager AI tool rollout, AI-native hiring Status report drafting

docs.title: AI Era — Backend Engineer
docs.text:
Bottom line: The most critical new layer is AI integration plumbing — LLM APIs, vector stores, agent orchestration. Architecture and security judgment matter more as AI handles more boilerplate.

New AI-era skills

  • LLM API integration (streaming, tools)
  • RAG architecture & vector DBs
  • AI agent orchestration (LangChain, LlamaIndex)
  • Function calling & MCP
  • Prompt engineering
  • Structured outputs (JSON mode)

Amplified importance

  • API design & contracts
  • System architecture
  • Security & auth
  • Database optimization
  • Observability & tracing

Still essential

  • A primary language (Python, Go, Node.js)
  • SQL & NoSQL
  • Docker
  • Auth patterns
  • Unit testing

Increasingly automated

  • Boilerplate CRUD endpoints
  • Basic DB migrations
  • Repetitive utility scripts
  • Simple middleware

docs.title: What this means in practice
docs.text:
Backend engineers are now expected to build the plumbing that connects products to AI models. This means understanding vector databases, embedding pipelines, streaming APIs, and how to design systems that degrade gracefully when an LLM returns unexpected output.

The engineers getting hired at a premium right now are those who can build a production-grade RAG system, instrument it with proper observability, and reason about latency and cost tradeoffs — not just call openai.chat.completions.create().

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:

  • Deep Dive — Backend

docs.title: AI Era — Data / ML Engineer
docs.text:
Bottom line: This is the highest-demand path in the AI era. Traditional ML still matters but GenAI skills — fine-tuning, RAG, evals, agents — are now essential in every ML role.

New AI-era skills

  • Fine-tuning & RLHF
  • RAG pipeline design
  • LLM evals & benchmarking
  • AI agents & tool use
  • Prompt engineering (systematic)
  • Multimodal models (vision, audio)
  • Synthetic data generation

Amplified importance

  • MLOps & experiment tracking
  • Python (deeper)
  • Statistics & probability
  • Feature stores
  • Model interpretability (SHAP, LIME)
  • Data quality & pipelines

Still essential

  • PyTorch / TensorFlow
  • SQL & Spark
  • Pandas / NumPy
  • Scikit-learn
  • Jupyter / notebooks

Increasingly automated

  • Standard EDA templates
  • Basic feature engineering
  • Routine model evaluation scripts
  • Boilerplate training loops

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Roadmap — Data / ML
  • Deep Dive — Data / ML

docs.title: AI Era — DevOps / Cloud Engineer
docs.text:
Bottom line: AI/ML infra is a new discipline within DevOps. GPU cluster management, model serving, and LLMOps are fast becoming core skills alongside traditional reliability engineering.

New AI-era skills

  • GPU & ML infrastructure (CUDA, NCCL)
  • Model serving (vLLM, TorchServe, Triton)
  • LLMOps (experiment tracking, evals)
  • Vector DB ops (Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate)
  • AI cost optimization (FinOps for inference)
  • Inference pipeline observability

Amplified importance

  • Platform engineering
  • Security & compliance (SOC2, zero trust)
  • SRE & SLOs
  • GitOps (ArgoCD)
  • Kubernetes (multi-tenant)

Still essential

  • Terraform / Pulumi
  • Docker & K8s basics
  • AWS / GCP / Azure
  • Prometheus & Grafana
  • Linux & Bash

Increasingly automated

  • Boilerplate IaC templates
  • Standard pipeline configs
  • Routine runbook tasks
  • Basic alerting setup

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Roadmap — DevOps
  • Deep Dive — DevOps

docs.title: AI Era — Engineering Manager
docs.text:
Bottom line: Managers who understand AI capabilities can unlock 10x productivity gains from their teams. The skill gap between AI-native and non-AI-native engineering managers is widening rapidly.

New AI-era skills

  • AI tool evaluation & rollout
  • Managing AI-assisted development workflows
  • Build vs buy for AI features
  • Measuring AI productivity impact
  • Prompt engineering (for team prompts & evals)
  • AI ethics & responsible deployment decisions

Amplified importance

  • Technical strategy & architecture review
  • Hiring for AI-era skill profiles
  • Stakeholder communication on AI timelines
  • OKR setting for AI initiatives

Still essential

  • 1:1s & coaching
  • Sprint planning
  • Performance reviews
  • Cross-team coordination
  • Roadmap management

Increasingly automated

  • Status report drafting
  • Meeting summarization
  • Repetitive HR documentation
  • Standard onboarding materials

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Roadmap — Eng Manager
  • Deep Dive — Eng Manager

docs.title: AI Era — Frontend Engineer
docs.text:
Bottom line: The bar for shipping UI has risen. AI can generate layouts fast — your edge is taste, judgment, and building features around AI (chat UIs, streaming, agentic interfaces).

New AI-era skills

  • AI UI tools (v0, Bolt, Cursor)
  • Streaming UI patterns
  • LLM API integration
  • Designing chat interfaces
  • AI-powered search & autocomplete
  • React Server Components for AI

Amplified importance

  • System design thinking
  • Accessibility & WCAG
  • Performance optimization
  • TypeScript
  • Testing (e2e)
  • UX judgment

Still essential

  • React / Vue
  • CSS & responsive design
  • REST / GraphQL
  • Git

Increasingly automated

  • Boilerplate component code
  • Basic CRUD forms
  • Initial scaffolding
  • Copy-paste CSS patterns

docs.title: What this means in practice
docs.text:
The frontend role is bifurcating. Engineers who use AI tools to ship faster and retain sharp judgment about what good UI looks like will thrive. Engineers who either resist AI tools entirely or blindly ship AI-generated code without critique will struggle.

The most valuable new skill is building AI-native UI patterns — streaming text responses, chat interfaces, multi-step agentic flows, and real-time collaborative features. These don't have established patterns yet, which means engineers who figure them out now are writing the playbook.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Roadmap — Frontend
  • Deep Dive — Frontend

docs.title: AI Era — Full Stack Engineer
docs.text:
Bottom line: Full stack engineers who can integrate AI features end-to-end are the most in-demand profile right now. Product thinking + AI plumbing = rare and valuable.

New AI-era skills

  • AI feature integration (chat, search, generation)
  • Streaming responses (SSE, WebSockets)
  • Vector search integration
  • Prompt management
  • AI-powered user flows
  • LLM cost & latency optimization

Amplified importance

  • Product thinking
  • End-to-end system design
  • TypeScript
  • Security best practices
  • Performance profiling

Still essential

  • React
  • Node.js / Python backend
  • PostgreSQL
  • Docker
  • CI/CD basics

Increasingly automated

  • Boilerplate CRUD
  • Basic auth flows
  • Standard UI scaffolding
  • Repetitive migrations

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Roadmap — Full Stack
  • Deep Dive — Full Stack

docs.title: AI Era — Mobile Engineer
docs.text:
Bottom line: On-device AI is a major differentiator for mobile apps. Engineers who can run models locally (privacy-first, low-latency) and design AI-native mobile UX are pulling ahead.

New AI-era skills

  • On-device ML (CoreML, TensorFlow Lite, ExecuTorch)
  • AR + AI (ARKit / ARCore + vision models)
  • Local LLMs on device
  • AI-powered UX patterns (smart suggestions, auto-fill)
  • Voice AI integration (Whisper, speech APIs)
  • LLM API integration in mobile apps

Amplified importance

  • Performance & battery optimization
  • Privacy-first architecture
  • Accessibility
  • Native module development
  • App security

Still essential

  • Swift / Kotlin
  • SwiftUI / Jetpack Compose
  • REST API integration
  • Push notifications
  • App Store / Play Store ops

Increasingly automated

  • Boilerplate list / detail screens
  • Basic form validation
  • Standard navigation patterns
  • Routine API wrappers

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Roadmap — Mobile
  • Deep Dive — Mobile

docs.title: AI Era — Security Engineer
docs.text:
Bottom line: AI has created an entirely new attack surface: prompt injection, model exfiltration, jailbreaks, and adversarial inputs. AI red teaming is one of the fastest-growing security specializations.

New AI-era skills

  • LLM security & prompt injection defense
  • AI red teaming
  • Model extraction & inversion attacks
  • RAG poisoning & data security
  • AI governance & compliance (EU AI Act)
  • Adversarial ML
  • OWASP LLM Top 10

Amplified importance

  • Threat modeling (AI systems)
  • API security
  • Zero trust architecture
  • Supply chain security
  • Incident response (AI incidents)

Still essential

  • Networking & protocols
  • Linux & Bash
  • OWASP Top 10 (web)
  • Burp Suite
  • Cloud security (IAM)

Increasingly automated

  • Routine vulnerability scans
  • Standard compliance checklists
  • Basic SAST/DAST runs
  • Boilerplate security configs

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Roadmap — Security
  • Deep Dive — Security

docs.title: Deep Dive — Overview
docs.text: The complete picture for each path: career levels, tools, learning resources, interview prep, and salary ranges.

docs.title: How to use this section
docs.text:
Each path page has five sections:

Section What you'll find Career levels What's expected at Junior, Mid, Senior, and Staff/Principal — skills and focus areas Tools Core tools (use daily) vs. useful-to-know tools, organized by category Learning path Sequenced, opinionated resource list with time estimates and cost Interviews The actual topics that come up, organized by category Roles & salary Job titles, years of experience, and approximate US compensation

Salary data

Ranges are approximate USD figures for the US market (2024–2025). Actual compensation varies significantly by company, location, and leveling system. FAANG/top-tier startups typically pay 30–60% above these ranges. Non-tech companies typically pay 20–40% below.

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. Master a backend language (2–3 months · Free/Paid) Python with FastAPI or Go are highest ROI in 2025. Build a real REST API project.

  2. SQL & database design (1–2 months · Mixed) Mode Analytics SQL tutorial + "Designing Data-Intensive Applications" (Kleppmann).

  3. Distributed systems concepts (2–3 months · Paid) "Designing Data-Intensive Applications" is the must-read. Supplement with Martin Fowler blog.

  4. Docker & containerization (2–3 weeks · Free) Play With Docker + official docs. Build, ship, and run your app in containers.

  5. Cloud fundamentals (AWS/GCP) (1–2 months · Paid) AWS Solutions Architect Associate or GCP Associate Cloud Engineer cert.

  6. System design (Ongoing · Paid) ByteByteGo by Alex Xu — covers real interview scenarios and production patterns.

  7. AI integration (LLM APIs) (2–3 weeks · Free) Anthropic & OpenAI cookbooks + build a RAG system from scratch.

  • Event-driven architecture
  • Microservices tradeoffs

docs.text: Core PythonDeep learningMLOpsData pipelinesDatabasesGenAI stack Tool Priority Pandas ⭐ Core NumPy ⭐ Core Scikit-learn ⭐ Core Matplotlib ⭐ Core Polars Useful Tool Priority PyTorch ⭐ Core Hugging Face Transformers ⭐ Core TensorFlow / Keras Useful JAX Useful Tool Priority MLflow ⭐ Core Weights & Biases ⭐ Core DVC Useful Kubeflow Useful BentoML Useful Tool Priority Apache Airflow ⭐ Core dbt ⭐ Core Spark Useful Prefect Useful Dagster Useful Tool Priority PostgreSQL + pgvector ⭐ Core Pinecone Useful Snowflake Useful BigQuery Useful Qdrant Useful Tool Priority OpenAI / Anthropic API ⭐ Core LangChain / LlamaIndex ⭐ Core vLLM Useful Ollama Useful

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. Python + statistics foundation (2–3 months · Mixed) "Python for Data Analysis" (Wes McKinney) + Khan Academy Statistics. Non-negotiable.

  2. Machine learning fundamentals (2–3 months · Paid) Andrew Ng's ML Specialization on Coursera. Best structured intro available.

  3. Deep learning with PyTorch (2–3 months · Free) "Deep Learning with PyTorch" (official book, free) + fast.ai Practical DL course.

  4. Hugging Face & transformers (3–4 weeks · Free) Hugging Face NLP course (free). Build fine-tuned models on real datasets.

  5. MLOps fundamentals (1–2 months · Free) Full Stack Deep Learning (free) — covers deployment, monitoring, and MLOps.

  6. LLMs & RAG systems (1 month · Paid) DeepLearning.AI short courses (LangChain, RAG, Agents). Build 3 real GenAI projects.

  7. SQL + data warehousing (3–4 weeks · Free) Mode Analytics SQL school + dbt fundamentals. Essential for data-adjacent roles.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Containers & orchestrationIaCCI/CDObservabilityCloudAI/ML infra Tool Priority Docker ⭐ Core Kubernetes ⭐ Core Helm ⭐ Core containerd Useful k3s Useful Tool Priority Terraform ⭐ Core Pulumi Useful Ansible Useful AWS CDK Useful Crossplane Useful Tool Priority GitHub Actions ⭐ Core ArgoCD ⭐ Core Jenkins Useful Flux Useful Tekton Useful Tool Priority Prometheus ⭐ Core Grafana ⭐ Core OpenTelemetry ⭐ Core Datadog Useful Loki Useful Tool Priority AWS (EKS, RDS, S3) ⭐ Core GCP Useful Azure Useful Cloudflare Useful Tool Priority vLLM Useful Triton Inference Server Useful Kubeflow Useful NVIDIA CUDA Useful

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. Linux fundamentals (1–2 months · Free) "The Linux Command Line" book (free online). Practice daily — everything runs on Linux.

  2. Docker & containers (2–3 weeks · Free) Official Docker getting started + build and ship a real app. Understand layers and networking.

  3. Kubernetes (CKA certification) (2–3 months · Paid) Killer.sh + KodeKloud. CKA is the gold-standard cert for K8s practitioners.

  4. Terraform (IaC) (3–4 weeks · Free) HashiCorp official tutorials + build a real AWS environment with VPC, EC2, RDS.

  5. AWS / GCP certification (2–3 months · Paid) AWS Solutions Architect Associate or GCP Professional Cloud Architect.

  6. Observability (Prometheus + Grafana) (2–3 weeks · Free) Grafana Labs tutorials + instrument a real service with custom metrics and dashboards.

  7. SRE practices (Ongoing · Free) "Site Reliability Engineering" (Google SRE book) — free online. Understand SLOs/SLAs/error budgets.

docs.title: Deep Dive — Engineering Manager
docs.text:
Prerequisite

Engineering management requires real IC experience as its foundation. Spend 3–5 years as a senior+ engineer before making this transition.

docs.title: Career levels
docs.text:
New Manager (0–2 yrs managing)Manager (2–5 yrs managing)Senior Manager (5–10 yrs managing)Director / VP Engineering (10+ yrs managing)

Focus: Transition from IC, build trust with reports, run effective 1:1s, improve team process.

Structured 1:1 frameworks Agile / sprint facilitation Technical code review awareness Feedback models (SBI) Hiring & leveling basics Conflict resolution Roadmap basics

Focus: Own team health and delivery, hire great engineers, manage up and across.

Performance management cycles OKR setting Stakeholder management Technical strategy input Hiring pipelines Team health metrics Org design basics Budget awareness

Focus: Lead multiple teams or a large team, set technical direction, partner with product leadership.

Managing managers Cross-org program coordination Technical roadmap ownership Engineering culture Headcount & budget planning Executive communication AI tool adoption strategy

Focus: Own engineering org, define multi-year strategy, build culture, work with board / investors.

Org design & team topology M&A technical diligence Company-level technical vision Engineering brand & recruiting Build vs buy at org scale P&L awareness AI transformation leadership Board-level communication

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Planning & trackingCommunicationPeople & HRMetrics & dataAI tools for managers Tool Priority Jira ⭐ Core Linear ⭐ Core Notion ⭐ Core Asana Useful Shortcut Useful Tool Priority Slack ⭐ Core Loom ⭐ Core Notion (docs) ⭐ Core Confluence Useful Tool Priority Lattice Useful Workday Useful Rippling Useful Culture Amp Useful Tool Priority DORA metrics ⭐ Core LinearB / Swarmia Useful Amplitude Useful Looker Useful Tool Priority Meeting AI (Otter, Fireflies) ⭐ Core Claude / ChatGPT (writing) ⭐ Core Cursor (code review support) Useful GitHub Copilot (team metrics) Useful

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. Be an excellent IC first (3–5 yrs · Experience) The best managers have strong technical credibility. Spend 3–5 years as a senior+ engineer.

  2. "The Manager's Path" by Camille Fournier (1 week · Book) The canonical eng management book. Read it before making the switch and re-read every year.

  3. "An Elegant Puzzle" by Will Larson (1 week · Book) Covers systems thinking for engineering orgs. Invaluable for senior managers and directors.

  4. Structured 1:1 frameworks (Ongoing · Free) Lara Hogan's resources (larahogan.me) — practical, research-backed management techniques.

  5. OKR methodology (1 week · Book) Christina Wodtke's "Radical Focus" — how to set and execute meaningful OKRs.

  6. AI tools for engineering teams (Ongoing · Experience) Learn hands-on: use Cursor, Copilot, and Claude daily. Understand what your engineers experience.

  7. Finance & headcount basics (Ongoing · Experience) Learn how P&L, headcount planning, and budget cycles work. Talk to your finance partner.

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. HTML, CSS & JS fundamentals (2–3 months · Free) MDN Web Docs, The Odin Project, or freeCodeCamp. Non-negotiable foundation.

  2. React & component thinking (1–2 months · Free) Official React docs (react.dev) + build 3–5 real projects with hooks and state.

  3. TypeScript (3–4 weeks · Paid) Total TypeScript by Matt Pocock — best structured course available.

  4. Next.js & full-stack patterns (3–4 weeks · Free) Official Next.js docs + Vercel tutorials. Learn App Router, RSC, and deployment.

  5. Testing (2–3 weeks · Free/Paid) Kent C. Dodds' Testing Trophy approach — start with integration tests.

  6. Performance & accessibility (2–3 weeks · Free) web.dev/learn + Lighthouse auditing. Study Core Web Vitals and ARIA.

  7. System design for frontend (Ongoing · Mixed) ByteByteGo resources + build complex apps. Read about micro-frontend architecture.

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. HTML, CSS, JS + React (3–4 months · Free) — The Odin Project is a great free structured path.
  2. Backend with Node.js or Python (2–3 months · Free) — Build a REST API with a real database.
  3. TypeScript (both ends) (3–4 weeks · Paid) — Total TypeScript + ts-node for backend.
  4. Database design (3–4 weeks · Free) — SQL properly, then an ORM (Prisma). Understand indexes.
  5. Full-stack frameworks (Next.js) (1–2 months · Free) — Build a full SaaS app with App Router, Server Actions, and Prisma.
  6. Authentication deep dive (1–2 weeks · Free) — Implement OAuth2 from scratch once, then use NextAuth.
  7. AI feature integration (2–3 weeks · Free) — Add LLM chat, vector search, and streaming to a project.

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. Swift or Kotlin fundamentals (2–3 months · Free) Apple's "Develop in Swift" or Google's Android Basics in Kotlin — official, free, excellent.

  2. SwiftUI or Jetpack Compose (1–2 months · Free) Hacking with Swift (Paul Hudson) for iOS. Google's Compose pathway for Android.

  3. Networking & data persistence (3–4 weeks · Free) URLSession + Codable (iOS) or Retrofit + Room (Android). Build an app that fetches and caches data.

  4. App architecture (3–4 weeks · Mixed) MVVM, Clean Architecture, or TCA (iOS). Architecture determines everything at scale.

  5. Cross-platform with React Native or Flutter (1–2 months · Free) Expo docs + build a production-quality cross-platform app.

  6. App Store deployment & CI/CD (1–2 weeks · Free) Fastlane docs + set up an automated pipeline: test → build → deploy.

  7. On-device ML (2–3 weeks · Free) Apple's CoreML & Create ML tutorials. Build a real app that classifies images or runs speech-to-text.

Networking (TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS, TLS) Linux fundamentals Python scripting OWASP Top 10 Burp Suite basics Wireshark Basic log analysis CVE awareness

Web app pentesting Cloud security (AWS IAM, GuardDuty) SIEM (Splunk / Sentinel) Threat modeling (STRIDE) SAST / DAST in CI/CD Malware analysis basics Security code review Compliance (SOC2, GDPR)

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: PentestingSIEM / monitoringCloud securityCode scanningIdentity & secretsAI security Tool Priority Burp Suite ⭐ Core Nmap ⭐ Core Metasploit Useful SQLmap Useful Cobalt Strike Useful Tool Priority Splunk ⭐ Core Microsoft Sentinel Useful Elastic SIEM Useful Wazuh Useful Tool Priority AWS GuardDuty ⭐ Core AWS Security Hub Useful Prisma Cloud Useful Prowler Useful Tool Priority Semgrep ⭐ Core Snyk ⭐ Core Trivy ⭐ Core SonarQube Useful OWASP ZAP Useful Tool Priority HashiCorp Vault ⭐ Core AWS Secrets Manager Useful Okta / Auth0 Useful CyberArk Useful Tool Priority Custom red teaming ⭐ Core Garak (LLM probe) Useful PromptBench Useful Rebuff Useful

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. Networking fundamentals (1–2 months · Free) Professor Messer CompTIA Network+ materials (free on YouTube). Deep understanding of TCP/IP, DNS, TLS.

  2. Linux & scripting (1–2 months · Free) OverTheWire Bandit wargame + learn Python for automation. Linux is the foundation of everything.

  3. Web app security (OWASP) (2–3 months · Free) PortSwigger Web Security Academy (free) — the best hands-on platform for web app security.

  4. CompTIA Security+ or CEH (1–2 months · Paid) Entry-level cert widely recognized. Covers core security concepts and often required for compliance.

  5. Cloud security (2–3 months · Paid) AWS Security Specialty cert or GCP Security Engineer. Crucial as everything runs in cloud.

  6. Penetration testing (OSCP) (3–6 months · Paid) OSCP by Offensive Security — the gold standard pentesting cert. Challenging but highly valued.

  7. AI/LLM security (Ongoing · Free) OWASP LLM Top 10 + build a prompt injection test suite. New and rapidly evolving area.

  • Defense-in-depth for LLM apps

docs.title: Roadmaps Overview
docs.text: Each path is organized into four progressive stages. The goal is to be honest about what actually matters at each level — not a laundry list of every tool that exists, but the skills that move the needle.

docs.title: How to read these roadmaps
docs.text:
Foundation skills are non-negotiable prerequisites. If you skip these, you'll be building on sand.

Core skills are what you use every day on the job. These are the things hiring managers expect you to know.

Advanced skills separate mid-level engineers from seniors. You won't need all of these early, but you should be working toward them.

Specialization is what defines staff and principal engineers — deep expertise in one or two areas that teams can't function well without.

docs.title: All paths at a glance
docs.text:
Path Foundation Core Advanced Specialization Frontend HTML, CSS, JS, Git React, TypeScript, REST Next.js, testing, perf Design systems, micro-frontends Backend Language, SQL, HTTP Frameworks, Docker, auth Microservices, Kafka, K8s Distributed systems, event sourcing Full Stack HTML, JS, SQL, Git React, Node/Python, Docker GraphQL, CI/CD, security Architecture, mobile, SaaS patterns DevOps Linux, networking, Git Terraform, K8s, AWS GitOps, service mesh, SRE Platform eng, FinOps, chaos eng Data / ML Python, SQL, statistics Scikit-learn, pipelines, BI PyTorch, MLOps, LLMs GenAI, RL, real-time ML Mobile Swift/Kotlin, Xcode/AS SwiftUI/Compose, APIs Flutter/RN, CI/CD, CoreML AR, on-device LLMs, SDK design Security Networking, Linux, OWASP Pentesting, SIEM, cloud sec Threat modeling, red team AI red teaming, CVE research Eng Manager IC experience, communication 1:1s, hiring, roadmaps OKRs, cross-team, budget Org design, M&A, AI strategy

Salaries and tool recommendations

For detailed salary ranges, tools ecosystem, and a sequenced learning path for each track, see the Deep Dive section.

docs.title: Backend Engineer
docs.text: APIs, databases, and distributed systems. Systems thinkers who like working under the hood.

docs.title: Foundation
docs.text: Python / Java / Go / Node.js Git & GitHub SQL fundamentals HTTP & REST concepts Data structures & algorithms

docs.title: Core skills
docs.text: Web frameworks (Django, Spring, Express, Gin) PostgreSQL / MySQL Authentication (JWT, OAuth2) Docker basics Redis caching Unit testing

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: Microservices architecture Kafka / RabbitMQ Database optimization & indexing Kubernetes gRPC Cloud platforms (AWS / GCP / Azure) Distributed tracing

docs.title: Specialization
docs.text:
Distributed systems design Event-driven architecture CQRS / Event sourcing Service mesh (Istio) Database sharding High-availability patterns

Key insight

The most critical new layer for backend engineers is AI integration plumbing — LLM APIs, vector stores, and agent orchestration. Architecture and security judgment matter more as AI handles more boilerplate. See the AI Era page for the full picture.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• AI Era Skills — Backend
  • Deep Dive — Backend

docs.title: Data / ML Engineer
docs.text: AI, analytics, and machine learning. Math-oriented engineers excited about AI.

docs.title: Foundation
docs.text: Python SQL Statistics & probability Linear algebra basics Pandas / NumPy Jupyter notebooks

docs.title: Core skills
docs.text: Scikit-learn Data pipelines (Airflow, Prefect) Spark / Databricks Feature engineering A/B testing Tableau / Power BI / Looker

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: PyTorch / TensorFlow MLOps (MLflow, Weights & Biases) Model serving (FastAPI, TorchServe) Kubeflow / Vertex AI LLM fine-tuning Vector databases (Pinecone, Qdrant)

docs.title: Specialization
docs.text:
Generative AI & RAG pipelines Reinforcement learning Real-time ML (streaming) Model interpretability (SHAP) AI safety & alignment Cloud ML (SageMaker, Vertex)

Key insight

This is the highest-demand path in the AI era. Traditional ML still matters but GenAI skills — fine-tuning, RAG, evals, agents — are now essential in every ML role. The steepest math prerequisite of all paths: statistics and linear algebra underpin everything.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• AI Era Skills — Data / ML
  • Deep Dive — Data / ML

docs.title: DevOps / Cloud Engineer
docs.text: Infrastructure, reliability, and CI/CD. Engineers who want to make systems run smoothly.

docs.title: Foundation
docs.text: Linux & Bash scripting Networking (TCP/IP, DNS, TLS) Git Python scripting Cloud concepts (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS)

docs.title: Core skills
docs.text: Docker & container registries Terraform / Pulumi (IaC) AWS / GCP / Azure GitHub Actions / Jenkins Prometheus & Grafana Ansible

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: Kubernetes (EKS / GKE / AKS) Service mesh (Istio / Linkerd) GitOps (ArgoCD / Flux) Secrets management (Vault) SRE practices & SLOs ELK / OpenTelemetry stack

docs.title: Specialization
docs.text:
Platform engineering FinOps & cost optimization Multi-cloud strategy Chaos engineering Zero-downtime deployment Compliance automation (SOC2)

Key insight

AI/ML infrastructure is a new discipline within DevOps. GPU cluster management, model serving, and LLMOps are fast becoming core skills alongside traditional reliability engineering.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• AI Era Skills — DevOps
  • Deep Dive — DevOps

docs.title: Engineering Manager
docs.text:
People, strategy, and org design. Senior engineers ready to lead teams.

Prerequisite

This is the only path that requires real IC (individual contributor) experience as its foundation. It's a transition, not an entry point. Spend 3–5 years as a senior+ engineer first.

docs.title: Foundation
docs.text: 3–5 yrs IC experience Written & verbal communication Agile / Scrum facilitation CI/CD & code review awareness Giving & receiving feedback

docs.title: Core skills
docs.text: Structured 1:1s & coaching Hiring & technical interviewing Roadmap & sprint planning Jira / Linear / Shortcut Performance reviews Stakeholder management

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: OKR setting & tracking Cross-team program coordination Budget & headcount planning Technical strategy & trade-offs Conflict resolution Executive communication

docs.title: Specialization
docs.text:
Org design & team topology M&A technical diligence Build vs buy decisions Distributed & remote leadership Succession planning Product-eng partnership

Key insight

Managers who understand AI capabilities can unlock 10x productivity gains from their teams. The skill gap between AI-native and non-AI-native engineering managers is widening rapidly.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• AI Era Skills — Eng Manager
  • Deep Dive — Eng Manager

docs.title: Frontend Engineer
docs.text: UI, browsers, and user experience. Visual thinkers who love fast feedback loops.

docs.title: Foundation
docs.text:
The baseline every frontend engineer needs before anything else.

HTML5 CSS3 JavaScript ES6+ Git & GitHub Chrome DevTools Figma basics

docs.title: Core skills
docs.text:
The day-to-day tools you'll use on every frontend job.

React / Vue / Angular TypeScript REST APIs npm / yarn Webpack / Vite Responsive design SASS / PostCSS

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text:
What separates mid-level from senior frontend engineers.

State management (Redux, Zustand) Testing (Jest, Cypress) Next.js / Nuxt / Remix Performance optimization Accessibility (WCAG) GraphQL CI/CD pipelines

docs.title: Specialization
docs.text:
Deep expertise that defines staff and principal-level work.

Design systems Micro-frontends WebAssembly Progressive Web Apps Web animations (GSAP, Framer) SSR / ISR patterns

Key insight

Frontend engineering is shifting fast. AI can generate layouts and components quickly — your edge is taste, judgment, and the ability to build around AI (chat UIs, streaming responses, agentic interfaces). See the AI Era page for what this means for your skill priorities.

docs.title: Core skills

docs.title: Advanced

docs.text:
System architecture DevOps fundamentals React Native (mobile) Real-time (WebSockets) Monorepo tooling (Turborepo) SaaS product patterns

Key insight

Full stack engineers who can integrate AI features end-to-end are the most in-demand profile right now. Product thinking + AI plumbing = rare and valuable.

docs.title: Mobile Engineer
docs.text: iOS, Android, and cross-platform. Engineers who want to ship apps people carry everywhere.

docs.title: Foundation
docs.text: Swift (iOS) or Kotlin (Android) Git OOP & design patterns UI/UX mobile principles Xcode / Android Studio

docs.title: Core skills
docs.text: UIKit / SwiftUI Jetpack Compose REST API integration Local storage (CoreData, Room) Push notifications App lifecycle & permissions

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: React Native / Flutter (cross-platform) Unit & UI testing (XCTest, Espresso) CI/CD (Fastlane, Bitrise) App Store & Play Store ops Performance profiling & memory Offline-first architecture

docs.title: Specialization
docs.text:
Custom native modules AR (ARKit / ARCore) On-device ML (CoreML, TensorFlow Lite) Widgets & app extensions Deep linking & universal links App security & obfuscation

Key insight

On-device AI is a major differentiator for mobile apps. Engineers who can run models locally (privacy-first, low-latency) and design AI-native mobile UX are pulling ahead of the field.

docs.title: Security Engineer
docs.text: AppSec, pentesting, and AI security. Engineers drawn to adversarial thinking.

docs.title: Foundation
docs.text: Networking (TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS) Linux fundamentals Python scripting Cryptography basics OWASP Top 10

docs.title: Core skills
docs.text: Penetration testing basics Burp Suite SIEM tools (Splunk, Sentinel) Cloud security (IAM, GuardDuty) Vulnerability scanning (Nessus, Nuclei) Zero trust principles

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: Threat modeling (STRIDE / PASTA) Red & blue teaming Incident response playbooks Compliance (SOC2, ISO 27001) AppSec in CI/CD (SAST / DAST) Container & Kubernetes security

docs.title: Specialization
docs.text:
CVE research & responsible disclosure Security architecture review Malware analysis & reverse engineering Bug bounty programs DevSecOps tooling Digital forensics

Key insight

AI has created an entirely new attack surface: prompt injection, model exfiltration, jailbreaks, and adversarial inputs. AI red teaming is one of the fastest-growing security specializations — and almost no one is trained in it yet.
